sections.section_title: The Memory Wall and the Efficiency Frontier

focus_areas: iron law of ML performance and bottleneck diagnosis
GPU memory hierarchy and energy/bandwidth implications
batch size, workload placement, and prefill-decode decomposition
rationale.question_strategy: Use a full quiz that starts with classifying bottlenecks via the iron law, then applies roofline reasoning with chapter numbers, then tests how batch size and prefill/decode shift arithmetic intensity and optimization choices across subsections.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Begin with foundational identification of which term dominates, move to scenario-based roofline and memory-hierarchy reasoning, then end with an integrative comparison of prefill versus decode and the role of batch size.
rationale.integration: Connects earlier chapter concepts such as arithmetic intensity, batching, tensor parallelism, and the iron law of ML systems to this chapter's performance-engineering diagnosis framework.
rationale.ranking_explanation: This is the chapter's conceptual core: it introduces the governing performance equation, the memory wall, the roofline model, workload placement, and the prefill-decode split. It clearly warrants a full quiz.

questions.question: A profile of an H100-hosted LLM decode kernel shows 3 percent FP8 compute utilization and 92 percent HBM bandwidth utilization, with arithmetic intensity near 1 FLOP/byte. Which single change is most likely to translate into immediate throughput improvement for this exact workload?
choices: Move to a next-generation accelerator whose peak FP8 TFLOPS is roughly 2x higher but whose HBM bandwidth is essentially unchanged
Quantize the weights from FP16 to INT4 so that each weight read transfers one-quarter as many bytes per token
Rewrite the decode GEMM to schedule Tensor Cores more aggressively while leaving weight memory traffic unchanged
Raise the kernel's clock frequency budget in the launcher so that each arithmetic unit runs a few percent faster
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. The profile signature (low compute utilization, near-saturated HBM) identifies a memory-bound regime, so gains come from reducing bytes moved per token. INT4 weight quantization cuts weight-read bytes by 4x and directly attacks the dominant term. Each other option targets compute throughput while leaving the actual bottleneck (HBM bytes per token) unchanged, so a faster or better-scheduled arithmetic unit just idles more efficiently waiting for the same weights.
questions.learning_objective: Select the optimization family whose target term matches a profile signature of saturated memory bandwidth and starved compute

questions.question: Explain why increasing batch size is described as the universal control knob for LLM serving performance, and why memory capacity limits how far this strategy can go.
questions.answer: Increasing batch size amortizes the cost of loading model weights across more requests, so arithmetic intensity rises and decode moves closer to the compute-bound regime. For example, batch-1 decode is deeply memory-bound, but much larger batches can approach the H100 ridge point around 296 FLOPs/byte for FP8. The practical limit is KV cache memory: each extra request needs its own cache, so weights and per-request KV state must still fit in GPU memory alongside activations, which is why serving systems often hit a concurrency ceiling long before they hit a compute ceiling.
questions.learning_objective: Explain how batch size changes arithmetic intensity and justify the memory constraint that bounds its benefit

questions.question: True or False: If a workload is compute-bound on one GPU generation, memory bandwidth can be ignored when upgrading to a newer accelerator.
questions.answer: False. The chapter shows that ridge points rise across GPU generations because compute improves faster than bandwidth. A workload that was compute-bound on an older GPU can become memory-bound on a newer one, so bandwidth still matters when hardware changes.
questions.learning_objective: Evaluate how hardware-generation changes can shift a workload between compute-bound and memory-bound regimes

questions.question: Which statement best explains why newer GPU generations make performance engineering techniques like fusion and quantization more important rather than less important?
choices: Newer GPUs reduce HBM capacity, so every model becomes capacity-bound instead of bandwidth-bound
The widening gap means compute throughput rises faster than memory bandwidth, so more operations fall below the ridge point
Newer GPUs eliminate kernel launch overhead, so memory optimization becomes the only remaining concern
Arithmetic intensity is fixed by the model architecture, so hardware changes cannot alter the bottleneck regime
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. The chapter's widening-gap table shows ridge points climbing sharply across generations because compute scales faster than bandwidth. Workloads that once lived above the ridge point fall below it on new hardware, so techniques that reduce bytes moved (fusion, tiling, reduced-precision transfers) recover the utilization that compute gains alone cannot. Claims about shrinking HBM, vanishing launch overhead, or architecture-fixed arithmetic intensity misread how the ridge shifts.
questions.learning_objective: Compare hardware generations using ridge-point reasoning to predict bottleneck shifts

questions.question: A serving team is deciding whether to optimize prefill or decode first. Which pairing is most accurate for a single-request LLM workload?
choices: Prefill is usually memory-bound, while decode is usually compute-bound because decode revisits prior tokens
Both phases are equally overhead-bound, so CUDA Graphs should be the first optimization for each
Prefill is typically compute-bound because it processes the prompt in parallel, while decode is typically memory-bound because it reads weights to emit one token at a time
Both phases are typically compute-bound because transformer layers are dominated by GEMMs
questions.answer: The correct answer is C. Prefill uses large prompt-length matrix operations with high arithmetic intensity, so Tensor Core throughput matters most. Decode, especially at batch size 1, reloads large weights to produce a single token, so bandwidth dominates and memory-oriented optimizations are more effective. Treating both phases as overhead-bound or uniformly compute-bound ignores the structural difference between parallel prompt processing and sequential single-token generation.
questions.learning_objective: Differentiate prefill and decode phases and classify their dominant bottlenecks

focus_areas: kernel launch overhead and HBM round-trips
rationale.question_strategy: Cover both overhead reduction and memory-traffic reduction: one question on launch-bound execution, one on fusion categories, one on CUDA Graphs determinism, and one integrative question on why FlashAttention gains grow with sequence length.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Start with the basic motivation for fusion, then move through categories and CUDA Graphs trade-offs, ending with algorithmic reasoning about FlashAttention and memory complexity.
rationale.integration: Builds on earlier concepts of kernel fusion, memory hierarchy, arithmetic intensity, and attention from previous chapters, now applying them to concrete GPU execution patterns.
rationale.ranking_explanation: This section introduces major performance-engineering mechanisms - fusion, CUDA Graphs, and FlashAttention - with quantitative systems consequences. It warrants a full quiz.

questions.question: A profile of one transformer layer shows dozens of short kernels and repeated writes of intermediate activations to HBM. Why does fusing a sequence like GEMM → GELU → LayerNorm often speed up inference even though the mathematical function is unchanged?
choices: It reduces redundant HBM reads and writes of intermediate tensors and can also cut kernel launch overhead
It turns the layer from memory-bound into communication-bound, which GPUs handle more efficiently
It removes model parameters from the layer, so fewer weights must be loaded in future tokens
It forces every operation to run in FP32, eliminating numerical error from separate kernels
questions.answer: The correct answer is A. Fusion keeps intermediates in registers or shared memory instead of materializing them in HBM, and it replaces many small launches with fewer kernels. Claims about changing the workload into a communication problem or shrinking the parameter count confuse kernel structure with model architecture, and fusion neither changes numerical precision nor removes weights.
questions.learning_objective: Explain how operator fusion improves inference by reducing both intermediate memory traffic and dispatch overhead

questions.question: Order the following steps in FlashAttention's tiled computation: (1) update the running maximum and rescale prior partial results, (2) compute a local score tile from Q and K blocks, (3) accumulate the tile's contribution into the running output.
questions.answer: The correct order is: (2) compute a local score tile from Q and K blocks, (1) update the running maximum and rescale prior partial results, (3) accumulate the tile's contribution into the running output. The score tile must exist before the algorithm can compare its local maximum against the running one. Rescaling must happen before accumulation; otherwise partial outputs would be normalized against inconsistent maxima and the final softmax would be wrong. Swapping (1) and (3) would commit stale normalization constants and silently corrupt the exact-attention guarantee the algorithm was designed to preserve.
questions.learning_objective: Apply the logical sequence of online softmax operations in tiled attention

questions.question: Which workload is the best fit for CUDA Graphs?
choices: A research notebook where control flow changes every iteration and sequence lengths vary unpredictably
A decode loop with repetitive per-token execution, fixed shapes, and stable memory addresses across replays
An MoE model whose routing decisions change the executed operators for each token
A prefill service whose prompt lengths and batch sizes vary widely from request to request
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. CUDA Graphs work best when the operation sequence, tensor shapes, and addresses remain identical across iterations, which matches repetitive decode. Dynamic-shape prefill, routing-dependent MoE execution, and notebook-style changing control flow all break the determinism requirement, forcing expensive graph rebuilds that wipe out the launch-latency savings.
questions.learning_objective: Classify when CUDA Graphs are appropriate based on execution determinism constraints

questions.question: Explain why FlashAttention's speedup tends to grow with sequence length, especially compared with naive attention.
questions.answer: Naive attention materializes quadratic-size score and probability matrices in HBM, so its memory traffic grows with O(N^2). FlashAttention tiles the computation and keeps running statistics in SRAM, so it avoids those quadratic intermediates. At long sequence lengths such as 8K or 32K, that gap dominates execution time, frees memory for larger batches, and produces much larger wall-clock gains than at short contexts. The practical consequence is that long-context serving stacks treat FlashAttention as a prerequisite, not an optimization.
questions.learning_objective: Analyze how sequence length changes the relative benefit of tiled attention by comparing quadratic and linear memory traffic growth

questions.question: A kernel author is deciding between writing a fused attention variant in raw CUDA at the thread level or in Triton at the tile level. Explain why the tile-centric abstraction more readily exposes the SRAM reuse and fusion opportunities that FlashAttention depends on, given the chapter's memory-hierarchy framing.
questions.answer: The tile-centric model lets the programmer explicitly name and manipulate the block of data that must fit in on-chip SRAM, so the compiler can reason about which intermediates live in shared memory, which get spilled to HBM, and where cross-tile barriers are needed. Thread-level CUDA forces the author to hand-schedule warps, bank-conflict avoidance, and per-thread register usage across every stage, and fusion across several operations (scores, masking, softmax, output) quickly exceeds what a person can hold in their head. The practical consequence is that FlashAttention-class kernels are feasible to maintain in Triton but become brittle research artifacts in raw CUDA, which is why performance engineering teams standardize on tile-centric kernels when the attack is on HBM bytes rather than per-thread ALU usage.
questions.learning_objective: Justify why tile-level kernel programming better exposes memory-hierarchy reuse than thread-level programming for fused attention

rationale.question_strategy: Mix conceptual and deployment-oriented questions: one on outlier-aware schemes, one on PTQ versus QAT economics, one on operating-point-dependent quantization choices, and one on how precision changes batch-size economics through KV cache.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Begin with identifying the core quantization challenge, then compare methods, and end with a systems-level decision about deployment regimes and memory budgets.
rationale.integration: Connects earlier quantization concepts from prior chapters to large-model inference constraints, especially batch size, KV cache pressure, and memory-bound versus compute-bound operating points.
rationale.ranking_explanation: This section contains substantial new technical content and production trade-offs around quantization strategy, outlier handling, and memory economics, so it deserves a full quiz.

questions.question: Why do large language models often require outlier-aware quantization methods rather than a single uniform per-tensor INT8 scale?
choices: Because tensor cores can only execute quantized kernels if every hidden dimension has identical variance
Because a small number of activation dimensions can be much larger than the rest, so one global scale either clips them or wastes precision on typical values
Because LLMs always require activation quantization and cannot be served with weight-only quantization
Because block-wise scales increase arithmetic intensity enough to make decode compute-bound
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. The section emphasizes that a few hidden dimensions become extreme outliers; a single scale must either preserve those at the expense of most values or clip them and lose critical information. Claims about mandatory activation quantization or arithmetic-intensity shifts confuse the core numerical issue, and tensor cores do not require equal variance across dimensions.
questions.learning_objective: Explain why outlier features break uniform quantization in large language models

questions.question: A team needs to deploy a 70B model quickly and can tolerate a small quality drop, but they do not have budget for a multi-day distributed fine-tuning run. Which path best matches the chapter's recommended workflow?
choices: Start with QAT immediately because PTQ is only useful for small models
Use PTQ first with methods like AWQ or GPTQ, then escalate to QAT or QLoRA only if the resulting quality is unacceptable
Avoid quantization entirely and rely on CUDA Graphs to recover the lost throughput
Use FP32 weights first, then prune after deployment if latency is too high
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. The chapter recommends PTQ as the default deployment path because it is fast and operationally cheap, while QAT-like approaches are the fallback when aggressive low-bit PTQ misses quality targets. Jumping straight to full retraining ignores the engineering-cost trade-off, skipping quantization leaves the memory-bound bottleneck untouched, and leading with FP32 weights simply preserves the original problem.
questions.learning_objective: Evaluate deployment trade-offs between PTQ and QAT under resource and quality constraints

questions.question: Explain why weight-only INT4 quantization is usually more helpful for batch-1 decode, while weight-activation FP8 or INT8 quantization becomes more valuable at large batch sizes.
questions.answer: At batch-1 decode, the bottleneck is reading weights from HBM, so shrinking weight storage directly reduces memory traffic even if the GEMM still runs in FP16 after dequantization. At large batch sizes, the workload moves closer to compute-bound, so lowering both weight and activation precision can speed the arithmetic itself because Tensor Cores deliver more TFLOPS at lower precision. The practical consequence is that serving stacks often deploy different precision strategies for latency-oriented and throughput-oriented operating points, sometimes even on the same GPU fleet with request-level routing.
questions.learning_objective: Compare quantization strategies across memory-bound and compute-bound inference regimes

questions.question: True or False: Once paged attention eliminates KV cache fragmentation, quantizing the KV cache adds little additional value for batch-size scaling.
questions.answer: False. Paged attention reduces wasted allocation from fragmentation, but the cache entries still consume bytes for every stored key and value. KV cache quantization cuts those bytes directly, so the two techniques address different constraints and compound to increase effective batch size.
questions.learning_objective: Distinguish fragmentation reduction from byte-per-entry reduction when analyzing KV cache scaling strategies

questions.question: Suppose a serving system is limited by maximum batch size because KV cache memory crowds out request slots. Which change most directly increases the effective batch size?
choices: Increase the router capacity factor in an MoE layer
Use larger prompts so prefill does more parallel work
Apply CUDA Graphs so the CPU launches fewer kernels per token
Quantize weights and compress the KV cache so more requests fit alongside the model on GPU memory
questions.answer: The correct answer is D. The batch-size constraint here is memory capacity, not launch overhead or routing policy. Reducing both weight footprint and KV cache bytes frees GPU memory for more concurrent requests, which then raises arithmetic intensity and throughput. The MoE router adjustment changes which experts run, not how many requests fit; larger prompts aggravate KV pressure; and launch-overhead optimizations do not free a single byte of HBM.
questions.learning_objective: Analyze how precision engineering changes serving economics through memory budget reallocation

rationale.question_strategy: Test pipeline ordering, practical compiler selection, graph-break reasoning, and the boundary between fully automatic compilation and custom-kernel authoring.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Start with the core purpose of compilation, move to tooling distinctions, then end with trade-offs around dynamic shapes and deployment contexts.
rationale.integration: Reuses prior framework knowledge while extending it to system-level compilation, autotuning, memory planning, and backend-specific optimization choices.
rationale.ranking_explanation: This section introduces a major optimization layer with multiple frameworks, concrete pipeline stages, and practical deployment trade-offs, warranting a full quiz.

questions.question: Order the following graph compilation stages for a transformer block: (1) operator fusion, (2) graph capture, (3) kernel selection and code generation, (4) memory planning.
questions.answer: The correct order is: (2) graph capture, (1) operator fusion, (4) memory planning, (3) kernel selection and code generation. The compiler must first obtain a graph before rewriting it. Fusion changes tensor lifetimes and eliminates intermediates, so memory planning should reason over the optimized graph. Only then can the backend choose or generate kernels for the final transformed operations. Running kernel selection before fusion would lock in code generated against intermediate tensors that fusion will later eliminate, wasting compile time and producing slower binaries.
questions.learning_objective: Apply the logical sequence of graph compilation pipeline stages

questions.question: Which scenario most strongly favors XLA over torch.compile?
choices: A steady-state TPU training job with mostly static shapes, where whole-program optimization and automatic distributed partitioning matter
An exploratory PyTorch workflow with frequent graph breaks and changing Python control flow on NVIDIA GPUs
A low-volume inference service where compilation time must stay in the seconds range and models change daily
A custom attention kernel that needs manual algorithm design beyond compiler rewrite rules
questions.answer: The correct answer is A. XLA's strength is whole-program static compilation and global optimization, especially on TPUs and large distributed jobs. Dynamic exploratory workflows fit torch.compile better, low-volume services pay XLA's long compile times without amortizing them, and writing a new attention algorithm points to human kernel engineering rather than a different compiler choice.
questions.learning_objective: Evaluate which compiler framework matches a workload's hardware and execution characteristics

questions.question: A team enables torch.compile on a model with data-dependent Python branches and shape changes between iterations. What is the main performance danger if many graph breaks appear?
choices: Every fused kernel will fall back to FP32 arithmetic
Tensor parallel collectives will be disabled, increasing communication volume
The model will be split into many small compiled regions, limiting fusion and adding compilation overhead that may not amortize
CUDA streams will be forced into synchronous execution, eliminating overlap
questions.answer: The correct answer is C. Graph breaks fragment the trace into smaller subgraphs, so the compiler cannot optimize across those boundaries and may spend time compiling pieces too small to deliver meaningful gains. The other claims misattribute graph-break behavior to precision mode, collective scheduling, or stream execution, none of which torch.compile silently alters.
questions.learning_objective: Analyze how graph breaks can reduce the runtime payoff of compilation in dynamic PyTorch programs

questions.question: Explain why TensorRT often outperforms general-purpose compilers for stable production inference, and why many teams still start with torch.compile.
questions.answer: TensorRT can calibrate ranges, autotune kernels aggressively, specialize to exact deployment shapes, and optimize purely for inference without backward-pass constraints, so it often achieves higher throughput on fixed production workloads. Teams still start with torch.compile because it needs minimal code change, compiles faster, tolerates more model dynamism, and delivers immediate speedups during development. The practical consequence is a staged deployment: torch.compile for the weeks between model change and production lock-in, TensorRT for the months the model spec stays frozen.
questions.learning_objective: Compare deployment trade-offs between general-purpose and inference-specialized compilers

questions.question: A performance engineer needs a fused attention variant that computes an attention-score mask not available in any existing kernel library. Why is Triton usually a better starting point than a full graph compiler like torch.compile or XLA for this task?
choices: Triton eliminates the need to reason about SRAM budgets because it automatically chooses tile sizes for every kernel
Triton replaces the GPU's memory hierarchy with a flat address space, so HBM round-trips disappear by construction

Triton exposes a tile-level programming model that lets the author write a single custom kernel where fusion and on-chip reuse are explicit, whereas graph compilers can only rearrange and select among pre-existing kernel implementations
Triton guarantees higher throughput than CUDA for every workload because its scheduler bypasses the Tensor Cores
questions.answer: The correct answer is C. The chapter positions Triton as the right tool when the missing primitive is a custom fused kernel: its tile-level abstraction makes SRAM reuse and fusion decisions first-class, while torch.compile and XLA largely rewrite and schedule existing kernels rather than author new ones. The other choices overstate Triton's automation, misdescribe the memory hierarchy, or invent a throughput guarantee that contradicts the chapter's emphasis on Tensor Core usage.
questions.learning_objective: Select the right level of the compilation stack (graph compiler vs. tile-level kernel DSL) for a given optimization task

focus_areas: speculative decoding as a latency optimization under the chapter's memory-wall framework
acceptance rate as the deployment gate
interaction with batch size and compute saturation
rationale.question_strategy: Treat speculative decoding as prior-chapter vocabulary and build on it. Replace the definition-style item with an application SHORT that turns the mechanism into a deployment decision, following the §16 SHORT-4 gold-standard acceptance-rate pattern.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Start with an acceptance-rate-based deployment decision, then probe the misconception that low acceptance never pays off, and finish with integrating memory-wall reasoning.
rationale.integration: Reuses the memory-wall diagnosis from earlier in this chapter and the speculative-decoding mechanism introduced in prior chapters; tests how the two combine into a production decision without redefining either.
rationale.ranking_explanation: This section is short and mostly connective, but speculative decoding is a named learning objective and a major chapter idea, so it merits a minimal quiz rather than being skipped.

questions.question: A team deploys speculative decoding with a 1B draft model that proposes 4 tokens per step to a 70B target model. Production traces show the draft model's tokens are accepted only 25 percent of the time on their customer workload. Using the chapter's memory-wall framework, explain the system consequences of this low acceptance rate for throughput and for tail latency, and what it implies about whether speculative decoding should stay enabled.
questions.answer: Speculative decoding amortizes target-model weight reads across multiple proposed tokens: a single 70B verification pass commits up to 4 candidates at roughly the cost of one plain decode step. When acceptance exceeds about 75 percent, the amortization wins and effective throughput can rise 2 to 3 times because each HBM weight read produces several accepted tokens. At 25 percent acceptance, only one of every four proposals survives, so on average every accepted token still costs a full 70B weight read plus the wasted 1B draft work - the team is paying more HBM bandwidth than plain decoding while still running two models. Tail latency degrades in the same direction because rejected proposals force the target to re-emit from the last accepted position, adding a synchronization step absent from straight autoregressive decode. The practical consequence is that acceptance rate is a deployment-gate metric rather than a tuning knob: below roughly 50 percent on the production workload, speculative decoding should be disabled even though it was profitable in offline evaluation.
questions.learning_objective: Analyze how the acceptance rate of speculative decoding determines whether throughput and tail latency improve or degrade relative to plain autoregressive decoding

questions.question: True or False: On a serving stack that has already scaled batch size to the point where decode is close to compute-saturated, enabling speculative decoding reliably improves throughput because it replaces some sequential target-model steps with parallel verification.
questions.answer: False. Speculative decoding only helps when the target model has idle compute waiting on memory-bound weight reads, which is the batch-1 regime. Once the target is compute-saturated the draft model becomes pure added work and the verification pass competes for the same Tensor Cores, so throughput typically falls or stays flat.
questions.learning_objective: Evaluate the misconception that speculative decoding helps uniformly across batch sizes

questions.question: A serving team measures that plain batch-1 decode on their 70B model runs at roughly 8 percent H100 FP16 utilization and 88 percent HBM bandwidth utilization. Which of the following makes speculative decoding a more credible next step on this workload than an alternative precision or fusion fix?
choices: Speculative decoding raises HBM bandwidth above the hardware ceiling, which other techniques cannot

Speculative decoding exploits the workload's spare FP16 compute headroom by converting idle Tensor Core cycles into parallel verification of multiple draft tokens, effectively getting more accepted tokens per expensive target-weight read
Speculative decoding eliminates the need to store the target-model weights in HBM, collapsing the memory-bound regime
Speculative decoding is mathematically equivalent to INT4 weight quantization, so the team can skip it if they already quantized
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. The profile (saturated HBM, idle compute) matches the regime where amortizing weight reads across several accepted tokens wins. Speculative decoding trades spare compute for fewer effective HBM round-trips per emitted token. The claim about exceeding bandwidth is physically impossible, the claim about eliminating weight storage misdescribes verification (which still loads the target weights once per step), and the equivalence with INT4 quantization ignores that the two techniques attack different terms of the iron law.
questions.learning_objective: Justify when speculative decoding fits a diagnosed memory-bound workload under the chapter's iron-law framework

focus_areas: decoupling capacity from active per-token cost
how MoE shifts the dominant term in the iron law
system costs of routing and expert placement
rationale.question_strategy: Treat MoE as a named architecture from prior material and test its effect on the chapter's performance equation rather than its definition. Include a scenario that forces quantitative reasoning about active bandwidth vs. total capacity.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Start with a dense-vs-MoE serving comparison that requires bottleneck-shift reasoning, then analyze the trade-off, and finish with the common 'free scaling' misconception.
rationale.integration: Builds on earlier conditional-computation and distributed-systems vocabulary while setting up the inference-scale discussion of expert parallelism.
rationale.ranking_explanation: The section is concise, but MoE is a major learning objective and chapter-level optimization concept, so it merits a minimal quiz.

questions.question: A serving team compares a 1T-parameter dense FP16 model against a 370B-total / 37B-active FP16 MoE model with top-1 routing, and both run on the same H100 fleet under a tight inter-token latency SLA. Which statement best describes how MoE changes the dominant term of the iron law on the batch-1 decode path?
choices: Per-token HBM traffic drops from about 2 TB (dense) to about 74 GB (MoE-active), so the memory-bound decode term shrinks by roughly an order of magnitude even though total model capacity stays comparable
Per-token arithmetic intensity becomes hardware-independent, so decode is no longer either memory-bound or compute-bound on any GPU generation
Per-token HBM traffic is unchanged because every expert must be read for each token, so MoE only helps the compute term
Per-token HBM traffic rises above the dense baseline because routing tables double the weight footprint, turning a compute-bound workload memory-bound
questions.answer: The correct answer is A. Sparse top-1 routing loads only the active expert plus shared parameters per token, so HBM bytes per decode step fall from roughly the full model size to the active-parameter size. That attacks the memory-bound term in the chapter's iron-law decomposition and is the reason MoE can serve trillion-scale capacity on hardware that would stall on a dense model of equivalent quality. The claim that every expert is read contradicts sparse activation; the claim about hardware-independent intensity ignores that the ratio still depends on FLOPS and HBM bandwidth; and the claim that MoE increases per-token weight traffic inverts the mechanism.
questions.learning_objective: Apply iron-law reasoning to quantify how sparse expert activation reduces per-token memory traffic relative to an equivalent-capacity dense model

questions.question: Explain one major systems benefit and one major systems cost introduced by MoE layers, grounded in how they change the bottleneck rather than their definition.
questions.answer: The benefit is that sparse activation collapses per-token weight traffic to the active-expert footprint, so decode can fit on fewer GPUs or serve more concurrent requests at the same latency - the memory-bound term in the iron law drops by roughly the sparsity ratio without touching model capacity. The cost is that routing adds a control-flow step that can produce load imbalance across experts and, once experts are sharded across devices, requires all-to-all token shuffles whose communication volume can grow faster than the compute savings. The practical consequence is that MoE is most attractive at the latency-critical batch-1 regime, where the memory-traffic win is largest, and least attractive when communication-bound cross-node all-to-alls dominate.
questions.learning_objective: Analyze the trade-off between sparse activation efficiency and routing/communication overhead in MoE systems

questions.question: True or False: In MoE systems, increasing expert count is effectively a free way to scale capacity because only one expert is active per token.
questions.answer: False. More experts raise total memory needed to hold the model, complicate routing, and, once experts span devices, expand the all-to-all communication volume per token. Sparse activation reduces active compute per token, but total system cost still grows with expert count.
questions.learning_objective: Evaluate a common misconception about expert count as a free scaling knob

rationale.question_strategy: Test the condition for useful overlap, the role of batch size, and the CUDA-stream mechanism, ending with the practical caveat that overlap is not free.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Begin with the overlap condition, then analyze a batch-size scenario, then reason about asynchronous streams and interference.
rationale.integration: Connects distributed training concepts from earlier chapters with this chapter's optimization framing of the overhead term.
rationale.ranking_explanation: This section adds important distributed-performance reasoning and concrete system mechanisms, so it warrants a full quiz.

questions.question: Under what condition can communication-computation overlap remove communication from the critical path?
choices: When communication time is less than or equal to the compute time available to hide it behind
When communication uses FP16 tensors instead of FP32 tensors
When all collectives are launched after the backward pass completes
When the network bandwidth exceeds peak GPU FLOPS
questions.answer: The correct answer is A. Overlap only eliminates exposed communication when the communication finishes before the compute window it is hiding behind ends. Lower precision shrinks communication volume but does not by itself determine whether the transfer fits under compute; serializing collectives after backward defeats overlap entirely; and comparing bandwidth to peak FLOPS conflates two unrelated quantities.
questions.learning_objective: Analyze the timing condition required for communication-computation overlap to be effective

questions.question: Why does increasing batch size often improve overlap effectiveness in tensor-parallel workloads?
choices: It reduces the number of collectives per layer to zero
It makes communication launch overhead disappear completely
It shortens GEMM kernels so communication can start earlier
It increases compute time per layer, creating a larger window in which communication can be hidden
questions.answer: The correct answer is D. Larger batch sizes make per-layer GEMMs heavier, so there is more compute time available to cover communication. The chapter explicitly contrasts tiny batch-1 collectives, where software overhead dominates, with larger-batch cases where communication fits comfortably under compute. Collective counts and launch overhead do not vanish with batch size, and larger batches make GEMMs longer, not shorter.
questions.learning_objective: Explain how batch size changes the overlap budget in distributed execution

questions.question: Explain how CUDA streams enable overlap, and why overlap can still slightly reduce compute throughput even when it improves end-to-end step time.
questions.answer: Separate compute and communication streams let the GPU schedule GEMMs and asynchronous NCCL collectives concurrently instead of serializing them on one timeline. The catch is that communication still consumes hardware resources, including some SM capacity and data-movement machinery, so dense compute kernels may lose a few percent of throughput during overlap. That small interference is usually worth it because it hides far more communication time than it costs, which is why overlap remains the default training-stack optimization despite the measurable per-kernel slowdown.
questions.learning_objective: Explain the mechanism and resource trade-off of stream-based asynchronous overlap

questions.question: True or False: If communication is overlapped with compute, it no longer consumes any GPU resources and therefore cannot interfere with kernel execution.
questions.answer: False. The chapter notes that overlap uses separate streams but still contends for physical resources such as SM capacity and communication engines. Overlap reduces exposed latency, not resource usage to zero.
questions.learning_objective: Evaluate a misconception about overlap as free concurrency

focus_areas: PyTorch profiler and Nsight Systems workflow
common bottleneck patterns and profiling feedback loop
rationale.question_strategy: Use a broad full quiz that spans hierarchy, metrics, trace interpretation, and diagnosis. Include one roofline-based diagnostic, one metric-selection question, and one workflow/feedback-loop question.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Start with the role of profiling, move to metric choice and timeline interpretation, then end with diagnostic synthesis and iterative optimization logic.
rationale.integration: This section ties the entire chapter together by turning roofline, fusion, precision, and overlap into a measurement-driven workflow.
rationale.ranking_explanation: Profiling is central to the chapter's thesis that optimization must follow diagnosis. The section introduces multiple tools, metrics, and diagnostic patterns, so it merits a full quiz.

questions.question: A batch-1 LLM decode workload on H100 shows 2 percent MFU but 85 percent memory-bandwidth utilization. What is the best interpretation?
choices: The serving stack is badly broken because both compute and memory utilization should be high simultaneously
The workload is memory-bound and may already be close to the hardware limit for decode, despite low compute utilization
The kernel is compute-bound, so the next step is to optimize Tensor Core scheduling
The profiler is likely wrong because MFU and bandwidth utilization cannot disagree by that much
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. Inference decode often saturates bandwidth long before it uses much of the available FLOPS, so low MFU with high MBU is consistent with a healthy memory-bound implementation. Treating low MFU alone as failure confuses training-style metrics with inference bottlenecks, and a profiler that shows divergent MFU and MBU is reporting the expected signature of memory-bound decode.
questions.learning_objective: Interpret profiling metrics correctly for memory-bound inference workloads

questions.question: Which profiling level is the right starting point if the only symptom you have is that end-to-end latency is 3x above the service SLO?
choices: Kernel-level profiling only, because the largest kernel is almost always the bottleneck
Application-level profiling first, then drill down through distributed and trace-level views to local causes
Immediately rewrite the attention kernel before collecting any traces
Skip profiling and compute arithmetic intensity from the model architecture alone
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. The section recommends a drill-down workflow from global symptoms to local causes. Starting at application level tells you whether the problem is queueing, communication, launch gaps, or a specific kernel, whereas jumping straight to one kernel risks optimizing the wrong layer of the stack, and model-architecture reasoning alone cannot expose runtime stalls.
questions.learning_objective: Design a profiling workflow that moves from global symptoms to local causes

questions.question: A LayerNorm kernel achieves about 2.8 TB/s on an H100 and has arithmetic intensity around 5.4 FLOP/byte. Explain what the roofline diagnosis is and what optimization class it suggests.
questions.answer: Because 5.4 FLOP/byte is far below the H100 ridge point, the kernel is firmly memory-bound, and the high achieved bandwidth shows it is already close to the hardware bandwidth ceiling. That means better FLOP scheduling alone will not help much. The right interventions reduce data movement instead, such as fusion to eliminate an HBM round-trip or lower precision to cut bytes per element, because the only way to raise effective throughput is to shrink the bytes the kernel has to move.
questions.learning_objective: Analyze profiler and roofline data to classify a kernel and choose an optimization class aligned with the diagnosed bottleneck

questions.question: In an Nsight Systems timeline, what pattern most strongly suggests kernel-launch overhead is a meaningful bottleneck?
choices: A few very long GEMM bars with no idle gaps between them
Many short kernels separated by repeated idle gaps and noticeable CPU-side launch activity
NCCL bars fully overlapped under long backward kernels
A memory timeline whose peak usage is flat and stable across iterations
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. Frequent short kernels with visible gaps indicate the GPU is repeatedly going idle between launches, exactly the pattern that fusion, CUDA Graphs, or compilation can address. Long continuous GEMMs, well-overlapped NCCL, and flat memory timelines each point to different bottleneck classes (compute-bound, communication-healthy, allocator-healthy respectively).
questions.learning_objective: Identify launch-bound execution patterns from a timeline trace

questions.question: True or False: Once an optimization improves a microbenchmark, it is safe to assume end-to-end throughput will improve by a similar factor.
questions.answer: False. The chapter emphasizes that optimization is a waterbed problem: speeding up a non-dominant kernel may barely change end-to-end performance, and fixing one bottleneck often exposes another. Verification requires reprofiling at the application level.
questions.learning_objective: Evaluate why end-to-end verification is necessary after localized performance improvements

focus_areas: difference between local-node and fleet-wide efficiency
rationale.question_strategy: Use a minimal quiz that forces fleet-level reasoning rather than metric definition. Open with a scenario where hardware utilization disagrees with MFU so the reader must pick the right metric.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Start with a scenario where busy GPUs hide low MFU, then move to diagnosing scaling regressions, and end with the operational reason stragglers matter.
rationale.integration: Extends earlier benchmarking, observability, and MFU concepts to fleet-scale optimization and regression detection.
rationale.ranking_explanation: This is a synthesis section with important operational concepts rather than a deep new mechanism, so a minimal quiz is appropriate.

questions.question: A 128-GPU training cluster reports 92 percent hardware utilization on every node, but MFU for the same run sits at 30 percent, a drop from 65 percent measured on an 8-GPU baseline. Which metric better exposes the fleet's actual efficiency problem, and why?
choices: Hardware utilization, because 92 percent is close to ideal and the MFU number is likely a measurement artifact of the larger cluster

MFU, because hardware utilization counts any time the SMs are busy, including cycles spent on activation recomputation, communication bubbles, and synchronization, whereas MFU counts only FLOPs that advance training
Neither, because both metrics track only compute and the real problem must be in HBM bandwidth utilization
Both metrics report the same thing at fleet scale, so either one is adequate
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. High hardware utilization with low MFU is the exact signature the chapter warns about: the GPUs are busy, but much of that activity is non-useful work like recomputation, padding, and pipeline bubbles. MFU isolates FLOPs that actually advance the model, so it exposes the scaling tax that hardware utilization conceals. Dismissing MFU as an artifact misses the defining fleet-scale diagnosis; blaming HBM bandwidth ignores that MFU already incorporates that effect through its denominator; and claiming the metrics are equivalent contradicts the scenario.
questions.learning_objective: Select the right fleet-scale efficiency metric given a scenario where hardware utilization hides wasted cycles

questions.question: Explain what the chapter means by the scaling tax, and give one reason it can increase when moving from a single node to a large cluster.
questions.answer: The scaling tax is the drop from strong local-node efficiency to lower fleet-wide efficiency once inter-node communication, synchronization, and variability are included. For example, a model that achieves high MFU on 8 GPUs may lose efficiency at 128 GPUs because InfiniBand latency and synchronization barriers stretch step time, and because gradient checkpointing or pipeline bubbles that were hidden on a single node are amplified by cross-node all-reduces. A sudden increase in that tax is a warning sign of scaling regression or infrastructure trouble, and the chapter treats it as the primary fleet-level health metric.
questions.learning_objective: Explain the concept of scaling tax and diagnose one of its fleet-level causes

questions.question: True or False: In a synchronous distributed workload, one node running 10 percent slower than the rest can reduce effective fleet MFU even if every other node is healthy.
questions.answer: True. The slow node becomes a straggler, forcing the rest of the fleet to wait at synchronization points. That idle waiting lowers effective cluster efficiency even though the majority of nodes are operating normally.
questions.learning_objective: Analyze how gray failures and stragglers propagate into fleet-wide efficiency loss

sections.section_title: The Optimization Playbook: A 70B LLM Case Study

focus_areas: interaction among quantization, fusion, batching, and speculation
rationale.question_strategy: Use a full synthesis quiz emphasizing sequencing and interaction rather than isolated definitions. Include one ordering question and multiple scenario questions about shifting bottlenecks through optimization rounds.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Start with the playbook sequence, then apply it to bottleneck classes, then finish with case-study interaction effects and deployment policy decisions.
rationale.integration: This section explicitly integrates the chapter's memory, fusion, precision, compilation, and algorithmic techniques into a systems optimization workflow.
rationale.ranking_explanation: The playbook is the chapter's capstone application section and deserves a full quiz that checks whether the reader can coordinate techniques rather than recall them independently.

questions.question: Order the first four steps of the optimization playbook for a new ML workload: (1) select the primary bottleneck category, (2) baseline measurement, (3) roofline classification, (4) apply the highest-impact optimization and verify.
questions.answer: The correct order is: (2) baseline measurement, (3) roofline classification, (1) select the primary bottleneck category, (4) apply the highest-impact optimization and verify. Measurement comes first so the team has real end-to-end evidence. Roofline analysis then identifies the regime, which determines the bottleneck category. Only after diagnosis should the engineer intervene and reprofile to confirm that the bottleneck actually moved. Skipping measurement and jumping to a bottleneck guess is the exact anti-pattern the chapter warns against.
questions.learning_objective: Apply the chapter's optimization workflow in the correct diagnostic order

questions.question: A serving stack is memory-bound at batch size 1. Which optimization sequence best matches the chapter's recommended ordering for large gains with modest engineering effort?
choices: Implement speculative decoding first, then profile only if latency remains high
Start with torch.compile, then apply weight quantization, then FlashAttention, then KV cache compression
Tune GEMM kernels by hand before collecting any trace data
Increase MoE expert count before reducing weight precision
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. The playbook explicitly recommends beginning with low-effort passive optimizations like torch.compile, then applying precision and library-level memory optimizations before algorithmic additions like speculation. Starting with hand-written kernels, speculation, or MoE architecture changes ignores the measured bottlenecks and sequencing logic.
questions.learning_objective: Select an optimization sequence consistent with the chapter's recommended playbook

questions.question: Explain why an optimization that succeeds in Round 1 can change which optimization is best in Round 2, using the 70B case study as an example.
questions.answer: Optimizations shift the dominant term in the performance equation. In the case study, INT4 weights and INT8 KV cache first reduced memory pressure and enabled larger batch sizes, which improved throughput but exposed attention, element-wise kernels, and launch overhead as the next major costs. That is why FlashAttention and torch.compile became the next best moves: after the first bottleneck moved, the optimization priority changed with it, and applying more precision tricks at that point would have yielded diminishing returns.
questions.learning_objective: Analyze how bottleneck shifts alter the optimal next intervention in an iterative workflow

questions.question: Why did the case study apply speculative decoding adaptively rather than unconditionally at all loads?
choices: Because speculative decoding only works when TTFT is above 500 ms
Because at high batch sizes the verification pass is no longer nearly free, so speculation can add compute overhead without proportional latency benefit
Because FlashAttention cannot coexist with speculative decoding in the same serving stack
Because CUDA Graphs require speculation to be disabled whenever quantization is enabled
questions.answer: The correct answer is B. The chapter stresses that speculation is most attractive at low batch sizes where decode is strongly memory-bound. As batch size grows and the GPU approaches compute saturation, verification becomes materially expensive and the trade-off worsens. The other choices invent preconditions (a TTFT threshold, incompatibility with FlashAttention, a CUDA Graphs constraint) that the chapter never states.
questions.learning_objective: Evaluate when speculative decoding should be applied based on operating point and bottleneck regime

questions.question: A team first applies INT4 weight quantization and sees a 3.1x throughput gain on their 70B serving stack, then applies FlashAttention on top and sees only a 1.4x additional gain instead of the 1.9x FlashAttention delivers in isolation. Which explanation best fits the chapter's warning that speedups do not simply multiply?
choices: FlashAttention and INT4 quantization interfere numerically because tile-based attention is incompatible with low-precision weights
torch.compile must already be enabled for FlashAttention to deliver any benefit, so the missing 0.5x is attributable to a disabled compiler

INT4 quantization already relieved much of the HBM pressure that FlashAttention was designed to eliminate, so the remaining memory-traffic savings are smaller and the stack is closer to the overhead-bound or compute-bound regime where FlashAttention helps less
FlashAttention's gains scale with sequence length, and INT4 quantization silently truncates context length, eroding the baseline FlashAttention was measured against
questions.answer: The correct answer is C. Both techniques primarily attack the memory-access term, so once one has moved the system away from the memory-bound regime, the other has less room to help - the same bottleneck-shift effect the case study illustrates. The numerical-interference claim contradicts production practice, the torch.compile claim invents a dependency, and INT4 quantization does not alter context length.
questions.learning_objective: Analyze how bottleneck shifts among memory-, compute-, and overhead-bound regimes make combined speedups sub-multiplicative

focus_areas: limits of compilers and precision reductions
rationale.question_strategy: Use a minimal synthesis quiz that checks whether readers can spot misleading mental models and avoid costly misoptimization.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Begin with the most common FLOPS misconception, then test a deployment pitfall, and finish with quality-aware performance reasoning.
rationale.integration: This section consolidates the chapter's earlier lessons into anti-pattern recognition across roofline, profiling, quantization, and compilers.
rationale.ranking_explanation: This is a synthesis and correction section rather than a source of many new mechanisms, so a minimal quiz is appropriate.

questions.question: A team upgrades from A100s to a GPU with much higher peak FLOPS but similar memory bandwidth, and batch-1 decode improves only modestly. Which explanation best fits the chapter's framework?
choices: The workload was memory-bound, so similar bandwidth means token generation rate changes little despite the larger compute peak
The new GPU likely disabled tensor parallelism, so all gains were canceled by communication
Speculative decoding must have been active on the old hardware and inactive on the new hardware
The larger FLOPS number only matters for training workloads, never for inference of any kind
questions.answer: The correct answer is A. The chapter's central warning is that batch-1 decode is dominated by memory bandwidth, not raw compute peak. Saying FLOPS never matters for inference overstates the point; large-batch inference can become compute-bound, but this specific operating point does not. The tensor-parallelism and speculative-decoding claims invent configuration changes the scenario does not describe.
questions.learning_objective: Evaluate the common misconception that more FLOPS automatically means faster inference

questions.question: True or False: If a graph compiler is enabled, manual kernel engineering and algorithmic innovation become unnecessary for top-tier performance.
questions.answer: False. The chapter explicitly argues that compilers automate known transformations, but breakthroughs like FlashAttention and speculative decoding required human algorithmic insight beyond compiler heuristics.
questions.learning_objective: Differentiate compiler automation from human-led algorithmic performance innovation

questions.question: Explain why measuring throughput alone can lead a team to ship a worse serving system, even if the tokens/second number improves.
questions.answer: Throughput can rise while answer quality, tail latency, or both get worse. For example, aggressive quantization might double tokens per second but degrade reasoning on hard cases, or a scheduler optimized for average throughput might create bad P99 latency spikes that break the SLA. The chapter's efficiency frontier requires judging performance jointly with quality and user-facing latency, not throughput in isolation, because a higher-throughput stack that misses the quality or tail-latency bar is strictly worse than the lower-throughput baseline.
questions.learning_objective: Justify why performance must be evaluated jointly with quality and tail-latency metrics

focus_areas: chapter-wide synthesis of roofline, fusion, precision, compilation, and profiling
matching software structure to hardware physics
rationale.question_strategy: Use a minimal synthesis quiz that checks whether the reader can restate the chapter's governing principle and relate major techniques to the terms they attack.
rationale.difficulty_progression: Start with the chapter's central constraint, then test integration across technique families, and finish with the iterative profiling principle.
rationale.integration: This section consolidates all chapter objectives and ties back to the fleet stack and iron law concepts introduced earlier in the book.
rationale.ranking_explanation: The summary is integrative rather than novel, so a minimal quiz is appropriate, but it still deserves coverage because it crystallizes the chapter's core mental model.

questions.question: Which statement best captures the chapter's unifying principle of performance engineering?
choices: Performance comes mainly from making hardware faster through larger FLOPS numbers
Performance engineering is mostly about reducing the number of model parameters, regardless of bottleneck
The goal is to match software structure to hardware physics by identifying the active bottleneck and reducing unnecessary data movement, overhead, or compute
Once a model is quantized, the rest of the optimization stack contributes little additional value
questions.answer: The correct answer is C. The summary repeatedly frames performance engineering as fitting computation to the memory hierarchy, bandwidth limits, and execution overheads revealed by measurement. The other choices reduce the chapter to one mechanism or ignore the diagnose-first discipline that the playbook, profiling, and fallacies sections all reinforce.
questions.learning_objective: Synthesize the chapter's central principle linking profiling, bottlenecks, and hardware-aware optimization

questions.question: Which pairing correctly matches an optimization family to the part of the iron law it most directly attacks, based on the chapter's decomposition?
choices: Graph compilation attacks the overhead term through launch-gap and scheduling reductions; operator fusion and precision engineering attack the memory-access term by cutting HBM traffic and bytes per element
Operator fusion attacks the compute term by raising Tensor Core utilization, while speculative decoding attacks the memory term by halving weight bandwidth
Communication-computation overlap attacks the memory term by increasing HBM capacity, while MoE attacks the overhead term by removing synchronization barriers
Graph compilation attacks the compute term by autotuning GEMMs, while precision engineering attacks the overhead term by reducing kernel launch counts
questions.answer: The correct answer is A. The summary organizes techniques by which term they reduce: compilers largely attack the overhead term (fewer launches, better scheduling, memory planning), while fusion and precision both reduce bytes moved through the memory hierarchy. The alternative pairings misattribute fusion to compute, misdescribe speculative decoding as a bandwidth halving, claim MoE removes synchronization, or invent a precision-to-overhead link that contradicts the chapter's decomposition.
questions.learning_objective: Classify optimization families by the dominant term of the iron law they target

questions.question: True or False: The profile-optimize-reprofile loop is mainly useful at the beginning of a project; once the first bottleneck is fixed, later reprofiling adds little value.
questions.answer: False. The chapter closes by emphasizing that every optimization shifts the bottleneck, so profiling is not just the first step but every step. Reprofiling is what tells the engineer what to do next.
questions.learning_objective: Evaluate the importance of iterative reprofiling in performance engineering
